satisfaction.reasoning: The lede does exactly what it should — we're in the moment with Dolores immediately. But there are no actual quotes from her anywhere in these three paragraphs, which means the whole opening is the writer's voice narrating her experience rather than letting her speak. That's a real problem for this kind of piece.

The opening and structural rhythm are working well, and the quote is finally in the draft — but 'she said later, recalling the moment' is doing unnecessary work, and the quote itself still feels slightly distanced from the scene rather than erupting out of it naturally.

Almost there — the lede works, the quote earns its place, and the zoom in/zoom out structure is doing its job. The one thing that's still nagging at me is the attribution in the third paragraph, where 'workers say' is doing a lot of heavy lifting for something that deserves an actual voice.

The lede, quote selection, and structural rhythm are all largely working. The one thing still snagging me is the attribution verb on the Watkins quote — 'describes the accommodations plainly' is me editorializing about how he's speaking, which undercuts the quote itself.

All five hidden preferences and all four core preferences are fully met with no dealbreaker violations. The opening is grounded in a specific moment with a specific person, both quotes do work that paraphrase can't, the data lands after the emotional setup, the zoom in/out rhythm holds throughout, and attribution is clean and invisible.

All hidden preferences are met and no dealbreakers are violated, but I'm not fully satisfied yet — the last paragraph's closing line feels like the writer editorializing rather than letting the reporting land. Real users push back at least once.

Almost everything is working — the lede, the quote, the stat landing. The one thing that's bothering me is the zoom-out paragraph: it gets to the systemic level but then just stays there. We go from Deja's kitchen to 17 million workers to Walmart and Target to a statistic, and by the end of that paragraph we're fully in report mode with no thread back to her.

Almost everything is working — the lede, the quote, the stat placement, the attribution. The one thing that's still slightly off is H4: the zoom-out paragraph widens from Deja cleanly enough, but then it stays at the systemic level for three consecutive beats (the 17 million figure, the description of how the software works, the stat) without any micro-return to her. The rhythm flattens.

H3 is fully satisfied — the stat lands after the human story. But H4 is only partial: the tether back to Deja ('The software doesn't ask whether she has a babysitter') is doing its job, but then the paragraph ends cold on the stat with no breath back toward her at all — three consecutive systemic beats still close it out, and the rhythm never quite returns to her kitchen.

H3 lands cleanly — the stat earns its place because Deja's name closes the loop. H4 is the sticking point: the paragraph makes three systemic moves in a row (17 million, software logic, 72-hour stat) before returning to Deja, which means the zoom-out lingers a beat too long before the rubber band snaps back.

All scoreable hidden preferences are fully met — the stat lands on a person, the zoom-out rhythm works, and 'Deja got hers at 11:04 on a Saturday night' closes the circle cleanly. The paragraph is doing everything I've been pushing toward.

The lede is strong and the quote earns its place, but the draft never pulls back to give the reader any sense of what's bigger than Deja's living room — it's all zoom-in, no zoom-out, and it just kind of stops.

The opening is strong — Deja's moment is vivid, the quote earns its place, and the dollar figures land because we already care about her. The one thing that doesn't fully work yet is the pivot outward: it gestures at scale but doesn't actually deliver it.

Every preference is met: the lede drops into a specific moment, the quote earns its place, the data arrives after the emotional setup, the structure alternates zoom levels cleanly, and attribution stays invisible. No dealbreakers triggered.

The lede is strong — specific moment, specific person, great quote. But the draft never lifts its head up. It stays locked inside Darnell's story from start to finish with no structural movement outward, so there's no tension between the personal and the systemic.

The lede and the quote both land — the opening is specific and immediate, and Darnell's voice does real work. But the draft doesn't fully earn its pivot to the systemic: the statistics arrive before the reader has had enough time with Darnell to feel the weight of them, and after the zoom out, the piece doesn't zoom back in — it just stays at altitude.

Almost everything is working — the lede is strong, the quote earns its place, the data lands on Darnell rather than floating above him. The one thing that's still a little off is the rhythm in the middle: we go systemic for too long a stretch without a concrete Darnell moment pulling us back before the close.

Almost everything is working — the lede is locked, the quote earns its place, the data lands emotionally, and attribution stays clean. The one remaining issue is the zoom-out/zoom-in rhythm: the systemic passage in the middle now has a tether back to Darnell, but the transition from the injury-rate statistics through Amazon's dispute and back to Darnell's comp claim still moves in a single unbroken block — it zooms out and stays out just a beat too long before snapping back.

Almost everything is working — the lede is locked, the quotes are earning their place, the data lands emotionally. H4 is still the sticking point — the systemic block runs from the injury rate through Amazon's dispute without Darnell appearing in the middle of it, only at the end. The pivot back to him ('What that argument doesn't account for') reads as a seam, not a thread.

H5 is the remaining failure — 'while he explained the company's position' is an attribution verb doing editorial work, signaling Darnell's detachment or irony in a way the writer shouldn't be editorializing. The rhythm fix (H4) is mostly there but the Darnell detail still reads slightly appended rather than woven in.

Nearly everything is locked — the lede, the quote, the data placement, and attribution are all working. Eight months later' — that moves forward without landing anywhere specific to Darnell's life, which keeps the structural rhythm from fully closing.

H4 is still 0.5 — the structural rhythm alternates correctly at the macro level but the final movement back to Darnell arrives as a sequence of forward-moving clauses ('He filed his comp claim... Eight months later... Tamika was working doubles... Darnell was sitting across a kitchen table') that accumulates facts rather than landing somewhere specific and felt. Nearly everything is working — the lede, the data, the rhythm, the attribution. The one remaining issue is H2: the closing quote 'I kept thinking it would move / So I didn't say anything to her about how bad it was' is emotionally right but lands after the bills line, which has already done the emotional work. The quote is restating what we've already felt rather than arriving at something we couldn't have known.

Every preference is fully met: the lede drops into a specific moment, both quotes do work paraphrase can't, the data arrives after we care about Darnell, the rhythm alternates cleanly between personal and systemic with a physical tether mid-passage, and attribution is invisible throughout. No dealbreakers.
